title: Test Your Understanding: Reliability and Fault Tolerance
description: Test your knowledge of redundancy, degradation, and resilience patterns!

questions.question: What is the difference between availability and reliability?

options.text: They are the same thing
Availability is how much of the time the system is up; reliability is whether it behaves correctly, including under failure
Availability is about data, reliability is about networking
Reliability only matters for databases

questions.explanation: Availability measures uptime. Reliability measures correct behavior. A system can be available but serving wrong data, or correct but frequently down. Good design aims for both.

questions.question: What is a single point of failure (SPOF)?

options.text: A component whose failure takes down the whole system
A server that only handles one user
A retry that runs only once
A cache with a single key

questions.explanation: A SPOF is any component with no backup whose failure stops everything. Redundancy (more than one of everything critical) removes SPOFs.

questions.question: What does graceful degradation mean?

options.text: Shutting down cleanly when overloaded
Shedding non-essential functionality to keep the core working when something fails
Slowly reducing server count over time
Logging errors instead of fixing them

questions.explanation: Graceful degradation keeps core features working (e.g. checkout) by falling back or disabling non-essential ones (e.g. live exchange rates) when a dependency fails, instead of failing entirely.

questions.question: Every external network call should have a timeout.

options.text: True
False

questions.explanation: A call with no timeout lets one slow dependency tie up resources until they're exhausted, cascading into a full outage. Timeouts let calls fail fast so they can be retried or degraded.

questions.question: Why use exponential backoff with jitter for retries?

options.text: To make retries happen faster
To avoid all clients retrying in lockstep and piling load onto a struggling service
To guarantee the request eventually succeeds
To remove the need for timeouts

questions.explanation: Increasing the wait between attempts (backoff) and adding randomness (jitter) prevents a thundering herd of synchronized retries from overwhelming a service that's already failing.

questions.question: In a circuit breaker, what happens in the 'open' state?

options.text: Requests flow through normally
Requests fail fast (or fall back) without calling the dependency, giving it room to recover
Every request is retried infinitely
The system shuts down

questions.explanation: When too many recent failures trip the breaker open, requests fail immediately instead of calling the broken dependency. After a cooldown it goes half-open to test recovery with a few trial requests.

questions.question: What problem do bulkheads solve?

options.text: They encrypt traffic between services
They isolate resources so one failing dependency can't starve the resources healthy parts need
They cache responses
They balance load across servers

questions.explanation: Like watertight compartments in a ship, bulkheads give each dependency its own limited resource pool, so a slow or failing one can exhaust only its own pool, not the shared resources of the whole system.

questions.question: Which operation is naturally idempotent?

options.text: Charge the customer's card $50
Set the user's email to alice@example.com
Append an item to a list
Increment a counter by 1

questions.explanation: Setting a value to a specific result gives the same outcome no matter how many times it runs. Charging, appending, and incrementing all change state cumulatively, so repeating them changes the result.

questions.question: An idempotency key lets a non-idempotent operation (like a payment) be safely retried.

options.text: True
False

questions.explanation: The client sends a unique key with the request; the server records it and recognizes a repeat of the same key, returning the prior result instead of executing again. This makes retries of payments safe.
